preFix.title: Laying natural stone floor tiles
preFix.subtitle: Set travertine, slate, or limestone on a prepared sub-floor
preFix.excerpt: How to lay natural stone floor tiles using a flexible adhesive mortar, including levelling an uneven sub-floor, back-buttering heavy slabs, grouting with a sanded joint compound, and sealing the finished surface.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Ceramic Tile Council guidance. BS 5385-4 (wall and floor tiling: natural stone). Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling.

content.text: Natural stone tiles (travertine, slate, limestone, sandstone) differ from ceramic and porcelain in two ways. They are heavy: up to 15 kg per 600 × 600 mm slab. They are also porous, so they must be sealed before and after grouting. Stone tiles vary in thickness within a batch by up to 5 mm, so
back-buttering
is not optional. A
flexible tile adhesive
is required throughout. Standard wall-tile adhesive will crack as the stone moves with temperature and the building settles.
Step 1, check the sub-floor

Natural stone demands a rigid, level sub-floor. A concrete slab is ideal. A suspended timber floor must be overlaid with 18 mm exterior-grade plywood screwed at 200 mm centres across the joists before tiling. Check the floor for level with the 1200 mm spirit level: any deviation greater than 3 mm over 1800 mm must be corrected with a floor-levelling compound before the adhesive goes down. Walk the area and mark any high or low spots with chalk. High spots are ground back with an angle grinder fitted with a diamond cup wheel; low spots are filled with the levelling compound and allowed to cure fully before tiling begins: typically 24 hours.
What you will need: materials

Flexible adhesive mortar rated for natural stone and large-format tiles (check the bag: not all flexible adhesives are rated for stone). Sanded tile grout matched to the joint width (5 to 10 mm for most stone). A penetrating
stone sealer

for the tile type being laid (travertine and limestone need an impregnating sealer; slate needs a slate-and-stone sealer). Tile spacers at the correct joint width. 6 mm or 10 mm joints are the working standard for most natural stone. Grout float, sponge, and bucket.
Step 2, seal the tile backs before laying

Before cutting a single tile, apply one coat of stone sealer to the back face of each tile. This is not standard practice with ceramic or porcelain, but with porous stone it prevents the adhesive moisture from being drawn unevenly through the tile, which can cause staining and ghost marks on the finished surface. Allow the sealer to dry for the time stated on the label: usually one to two hours.
Step 3, set out the tiles before cutting

Snap a chalk line down the centre of the room in both directions to find the working centre point. Dry-lay a row of tiles from the centre out to the walls in both directions to check the cut sizes at the perimeter. If a cut at the wall comes out to less than half a tile width, shift the centre line by half a tile to avoid a thin sliver at the perimeter. Mark the working lines with chalk and plan the laying sequence.
Step 4, mix and apply the adhesive

Mix the flexible adhesive to a stiff but workable consistency: it should hold a notch from the trowel without slumping. Use a 10 mm notched trowel to spread adhesive on the sub-floor, combing it in one direction. For a tile 600 mm or larger, also back-butter the tile: spread a thin even coat of adhesive over the back face with the flat edge of a filling knife, pressing it into the surface. The combined adhesive layers give 100 per cent contact coverage: the requirement for natural stone. Press each tile firmly into the bed with a slight twist to collapse the notch ridges, then check level with the spirit level before moving to the next tile.
Step 5, cut tiles

Natural stone cannot be scored and snapped with a manual tile cutter: it needs a wet-cut diamond tile saw. Hire or borrow one for the project; attempting to snap stone splits it unpredictably. Mark the cut line with a pencil and masking tape to protect the face. Cut at steady feed speed; let the diamond blade do the work. Slate, being layered, can sometimes be split along a cleavage plane but a wet saw gives a clean edge every time.
Step 6, allow the adhesive to cure, then grout

Leave the adhesive to cure for 24 hours before grouting, 48 hours in cool or damp conditions. Apply the sanded grout with a rubber float, pressing it into the joints and working diagonally across the tiles. Work in sections of about 1 m² at a time. Before the grout starts to skin, clean the tile face with a damp sponge in circular motions, rinsing the sponge frequently. Do not apply water heavily: overwetting the grout weakens it. Allow the
sanded grout
to cure for the time stated on the bag (usually 24 hours) before foot traffic.
Step 7, seal the finished floor

Once the grout is fully cured, apply two coats of stone sealer to the entire floor surface, including the grout joints. The first coat soaks in and raises the surface slightly; the second coat, applied after the first is dry, provides the protection. Polish with a clean dry cloth between coats. Travertine and limestone need resealing every two to three years in a busy kitchen; slate in a hallway can go five years between treatments.
Variations

Tumbled or aged stone tiles have an uneven surface and a rounded edge. They cannot be butted close; a joint of at least 8 mm is needed. Filled travertine has its natural holes pre-filled with grout or resin. Unfilled travertine exposes the voids and needs them filled with matching grout on installation. Polished stone (marble or limestone) shows adhesive bleedback through the tile face. For polished stone, use a white or light-coloured flexible adhesive and back-butter carefully.
Care

Clean sealed natural stone with a pH-neutral stone cleaner or plain water: never bleach, vinegar, or citrus-based cleaners, which etch the surface. Grit is the main abrasion risk; a mat at the door and regular sweeping keeps the surface in condition. Reseal on the schedule above. If the surface has lost its water-beading quality, the sealer has worn through and needs reapplication.

attrs.fix: If the tile is unfixed, lift it, clean both surfaces, and relay with full back-buttering and a thick adhesive bed. If the tile is grouted, it will eventually crack under foot traffic — relay it before that happens.
attrs.cause: Insufficient adhesive coverage; air pockets under the tile.
attrs.symptom: Tile sounds hollow when tapped after adhesive cures

attrs.fix: Apply a poultice of grout haze remover (pH-neutral formula for stone — not acid-based) to the affected area. Leave for the time stated, then clean. Seal the floor immediately after to prevent recurrence.
attrs.cause: Grout moisture was absorbed through unsealed tile backs, or cement residue was not cleaned from the face promptly.
attrs.symptom: Ghost marks or staining appear on travertine or limestone after grouting

attrs.fix: Rake out the cracked joints, clean, and regrout with a sanded flexible grout. Identify the source of movement — a timber sub-floor without sufficient plywood overlay is the most common cause.
attrs.cause: Sub-floor movement; standard (non-flexible) adhesive used; joints too narrow for the stone type.
attrs.symptom: Grout joints crack shortly after installation

attrs.fix: Prevention: back-butter thicker tiles more heavily and thin tiles less, checking level with the spirit level before the adhesive sets. If the tile is already set and the step is greater than 2 mm, the tile must be lifted and relaid.
attrs.cause: Natural stone varies in thickness by batch; tiles were not levelled individually during laying.
attrs.symptom: Adjacent tiles are not flush — one is higher than the other

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
